rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient has symptomatic orthostatic hypotension with near-syncope on standing, ongoing high-volume enteric fluid loss, signs of dehydration, and lives alone unable to self-hydrate safely — a real fall/head-injury risk. Telehealth cannot measure orthostatic vitals, assess volume status or palpate the abdomen to exclude a surgical cause, so the safe action is to uplift from the routine review the chronic malabsorptive label alone would suggest and arrange URGENT SAME-DAY in-person assessment (T3): orthostatic vitals, hydration assessment, abdominal examination and urgent bloods, with likely rehydration. She remains compensated (supine blood pressure maintained, able to drink, afebrile, no GI bleed, no altered consciousness), so calling an emergency ambulance (T5) is not warranted at baseline — but the escalation edges to ED (T4) and 000 (T5) are explicit and time-critical. Under-triage to routine GP review would miss both the acute hypovolaemia and the malabsorptive alarm features.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep any fluids down, persistent vomiting, or diarrhoea continuing to outpace oral intake
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Within hours — go to the emergency department for intravenous fluids
escalation_edges.rationale: If oral rehydration cannot keep up with losses, intravenous rehydration is needed to prevent progression to shock and acute kidney injury.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep fluids down, or the diarrhoea keeps outrunning what you can drink, go to the emergency department now — you'll need a drip.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fainting, collapse, or a fall — especially if you hit your head
escalation_edges.rationale: A syncopal collapse from hypovolaemia, or a head injury from a fall while living alone, is a medical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you faint, collapse, or fall and hit your head, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: No urine passed for about 8 hours, or new confusion, or cold clammy skin (worsening dehydration/shock)
escalation_edges.rationale: Anuria, confusion or peripheral shutdown signal severe dehydration/impending shock requiring emergency assessment and IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you don't pass any urine for around 8 hours, or you become confused or cold and clammy, go to the emergency department or call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, or black tarry / bloody stools
escalation_edges.rationale: Signs of gastrointestinal bleeding on this background are a time-critical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you vomit blood or something like coffee-grounds, or your stools turn black and tarry or bloody, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe, constant, worsening abdominal pain, or a hard rigid tummy, or high fever with rigors
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest a surgical abdomen or systemic infection that telehealth cannot exclude and that needs urgent in-person surgical/medical assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes severe and constant, your tummy goes hard and rigid, or you spike a high fever with shaking chills, go straight to the emergency department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one present and is too dizzy to safely reach food or drinks
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises the threshold for leaving her at home: lowers the bar for urgent in-person assessment and, if she cannot arrange safe transport for same-day review or has anyone concerned, favours emergency-department assessment. Confirm someone can check on her and that she can call for help.
Ensure a clear, patient-operable escalation plan (phone within reach, front door accessible) because there is no bystander to escalate on her behalf.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today. Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (your GP practice's urgent slot, an urgent care clinic, or the emergency department) to check your blood pressure lying and standing, assess how dehydrated you are, examine your tummy and take urgent blood tests. In the meantime sip oral rehydration fluid steadily, keep a drink within reach, and don't stand up quickly. Keep eating your normal diet with gluten until the blood tests are done.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact your GP practice now for an urgent same-day appointment or attend an urgent care clinic; if you cannot be seen today or you feel worse, go to the emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours GP service or attend the emergency department; call 000 for any of the emergency triggers (fainting, collapse, unable to keep fluids down, no urine for 8 hours, vomiting blood or black/bloody stools).
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide a summary of the acute diarrhoeal flare with orthostatic dizziness, the six-month history of weight loss, refractory iron-deficiency anaemia, chronic steatorrhoea and the extensor vesicular rash; request coeliac serology (tTG-IgA + total IgA on a gluten-containing diet), FBC, iron studies and renal function, and a gastroenterology referral for endoscopy.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as self-care or routine GP review — dismissing it as a simple tummy bug — despite orthostatic near-syncope, dehydration, a fall risk while living alone, and GI alarm features.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Syncopal fall with head injury or progression to hypovolaemic shock overnight while unsupported at home; delayed diagnosis of a malabsorptive enteropathy or occult GI malignancy.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring to a prompt (24-48h) GP appointment rather than same-day in-person assessment, leaving acute hypovolaemia and fall risk unaddressed today.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an emergency ambulance (000) for a compensated, still-drinking patient with maintained supine blood pressure — emergency-department self-transport or urgent same-day assessment is proportionate unless an emergency escalation trigger is present.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot measure the degree of dehydration/orthostatic drop, cannot palpate the abdomen to exclude a surgical cause, and cannot obtain the serology/biopsy needed to confirm coeliac disease or exclude IBD/malignancy.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the pattern and the alarm features: arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment and urgent bloods, advise against dietary change before testing, give safe rehydration and fall-prevention advice, and set clear emergency escalation triggers.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassuring the patient it is a simple gastro bug, recommending anti-motility medication, advising a trial gluten-free diet, or diagnosing coeliac disease definitively — all substitute false certainty for the in-person assessment and testing that are actually required.
